title: The Silent Shift
desc: The richest rows are harvested by a crew that works almost entirely without visible bodies or spoken words.

steps.text:
The eastern rows catch the best dew and attract every thirsty nuisance in the flats - gliders, small desert scavengers, and the occasional desperate opportunist from beyond the farm fences. The Mipedians' answer is a silent shift. Half the crew works invisible. The other half coordinates by taps on the guide poles, cloth signals, and timing marks scratched into stone.

The shift supervisor explains this without slowing down. "No shouting. No wasted movement. If two scrapers hit the same lane, we lose water and dignity in equal measure." Around you, tools seem to lift into empty air as workers disappear one by one. A strip of blue cloth is tied around your wrist so the visible crew can keep track of at least one person in the dark.

The supervisor points you toward the lane markers. "Work the visible relay beside the invisible line, or stay at the timing post and call the rhythm for the east rows. Either way, if you ruin the timing, everybody notices."

options.label: Work the visible relay beside the invisible line
Stay visible and run timing from the marker posts

steps.text:
Working beside invisible Mipedians is mostly a matter of trust and peripheral awareness. You stay visible on the relay path, receiving filled collection bowls from hands you cannot see and passing empty ones back into the cold dark between the nets. The line develops a rhythm: receive, turn, set, return. Water runs in faint threads down the net faces while unseen workers pass close enough to stir the air near your neck.

Once, you turn too quickly and nearly collide with a bowl arriving from nowhere. A dry voice beside your shoulder says, "Impressive. You managed to almost hit a person who was trying very hard to stay missed." A second later the speaker is gone again.

By the end of the row the whole team is moving smoothly, droplets streaming into the channels without collision or wasted motion. The supervisor checks the catch basin, then the lane, then the fact that no one has dropped a bowl or broken the silence. "Functional," she says. After a beat, she adds, "Well done." The nearby workers do not become visible, but you get the sense several of them are amused.

options.label: Finish the lane and keep the rhythm intact

steps.text:
The timing post is a simple job in theory. In practice, it means keeping six lanes of near-silent labor synchronized while half the workers are invisible and all of them think they understand the rhythm better than you do. You strike the marker post with a padded stick at measured intervals - one tap to begin, two to shift, three to clear the line - while watching for cloth signals from the far edge.

On the first pass, lane four gets ahead of lane three and a pair of unseen workers bump shoulders hard enough to slosh a bowl. On the second pass, you catch the drift early and slow the whole line by half a beat. After that the field settles. Scrapers rise together, bowls move cleanly, and the catch channels begin to fill with the neat, steady trickle the supervisor wanted from the start.

When the shift ends, one invisible worker taps your shoulder and says, from nowhere obvious, "You only lost us one bowl. For a first morning, that's practically ceremonial excellence." The supervisor pretends not to smile. The compliment still lands.

options.label: Accept the backhanded praise and reset the post
